title: Demand, Supply & Elasticity
description: Law of demand and supply, equilibrium, elasticity, and market movements

questions.statement: Demand is simply the desire to buy a good at any price.
questions.payload: Demand requires both **willingness and ability** to buy at a given price.

questions.statement: The law of demand states that price and quantity demanded are directly related.
questions.payload: The law of demand states they are **inversely** related.

questions.statement: A rise in consumer income shifts the demand curve to the right for a normal good.
questions.payload: Correct. Higher income increases demand for normal goods at **every** price.

questions.statement: A change in the good's own price causes a shift of the entire demand curve.
questions.payload: A price change causes **movement along** the curve; only other factors shift the curve.

questions.statement: When the price of bread rises, the demand for injera will likely shift right if they are substitutes.
questions.payload: Correct. If bread and injera are substitutes, higher bread prices **increase** injera demand.

questions.statement: At market equilibrium, quantity demanded is always greater than quantity supplied.
questions.payload: At equilibrium, quantity demanded **equals** quantity supplied exactly.

questions.statement: A price floor set above equilibrium creates a shortage in the market.
questions.payload: A price floor above equilibrium causes **surplus** because quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded.

questions.statement: If the government fixes teff price at 75 birr when equilibrium is 60 birr, a surplus will occur.
questions.payload: Correct. At 75 birr, suppliers want to sell **more** than consumers want to buy.

questions.statement: The demand for insulin is elastic because many substitutes are available.
questions.payload: Insulin has **no substitutes** and is a life-or-death necessity, so demand is inelastic.

questions.statement: Luxury hotel stays in Addis Ababa tend to have elastic demand.
questions.payload: Correct. Luxury hotels have many alternatives and are **not necessities**, making demand elastic.

questions.statement: The cross-price elasticity between coffee and tea is likely to be negative.
questions.payload: Coffee and tea are substitutes, so their cross-price elasticity is **positive**.

questions.statement: An income elasticity of 3 means the good is a normal good with strong income sensitivity.
questions.payload: Correct. A positive income elasticity above 1 indicates a **luxury** normal good.

questions.statement: An income elasticity of 3 tells us that price demand for the good is elastic.
questions.payload: Income elasticity measures responsiveness to **income** changes, not price changes.

questions.statement: Salt has inelastic demand because it has no close substitutes and takes a tiny share of the budget.
questions.payload: Correct. Necessities with no substitutes typically have **inelastic** demand.

questions.statement: When demand increases and supply decreases, equilibrium price will rise.
questions.payload: Correct. A rightward demand shift and leftward supply shift both push price **upward**.

questions.statement: A surplus occurs when the market price is below the equilibrium level.
questions.payload: A surplus occurs when price is **above** equilibrium, causing quantity supplied to exceed quantity demanded.

questions.statement: The law of supply states that higher prices lead to lower quantities supplied.
questions.payload: The law of supply states that higher prices lead to **higher** quantities supplied.

questions.statement: If the price of teff rises, we say the quantity demanded of teff has decreased.
questions.payload: Correct. A price change causes a change in **quantity demanded**, not a shift in demand.

questions.statement: Expectations of future price rises will shift the current demand curve to the left.
questions.payload: If consumers expect higher future prices, current demand shifts **rightward**.

questions.statement: Brand-name sports shoes have elastic demand because many competing brands exist.
questions.payload: Correct. Many substitutes and non-essential status make brand-name shoe demand **elastic**.
